q: Heat engine is a system that converts heat to mechanical energy, which can then be used to do mechanical work.
A series of processes in which a system is brought back to its initial state is called a cycle.
Thermal efficiency for a heat engine is the ratio of work output to heat input.
Applying the first law of thermodynamic to one complete cycle, there is no net change of internal energy ($\Delta U = 0$).
For a heat engine cycle, $Q_H - Q_C = W$.
Internal combustion engines generate the input heat by the combustion of fuel within the engine itself.
A gasoline engine is called a four stroke engine.
The gasoline engine cycle involves the performance of six processes, of which four require motion of the piston.
In the intake stroke, a mixture of gasoline vapour and air is drawn into the cylinder by the suction stroke of the piston.
In the compression stroke, the mixture of gasoline vapour and air is compressed until its pressure and temperature rise considerably.
Explosion of the hot mixture is caused to take place very rapidly by an electric spark.
The piston does not move during the explosion process.
During explosion, the combustion products attain a very high pressure and temperature, but the volume remains unchanged.
In the power stroke, the hot combustion products expand and push the piston out, thus suffering a drop in pressure and temperature.
An exhaust valve allows some gas to escape during valve exhaust until the pressure drops to that of the atmosphere.
In the exhaust stroke, the piston pushes almost all the remaining combustion products out of the cylinder.
The ratio $\frac{V_1}{V_2}$ is the compression ratio.
For a typical ideal gasoline engine with a compression ratio of 8 and $\gamma = 1.4$, the theoretical efficiency is $\eta = 56\%$.
The diesel engine induces only air into the combustion chamber on its intake stroke.
In a diesel engine, fuel is sprayed into the cylinder and immediately ignites because of the heat and pressure inside.
The diesel engine can operate on a two-stroke or four-stroke cycle.
Steam engine is operating between the steam temperature 373 K and outside room temperature 300 K.
Gasoline engine is operating between petrol burning temperature 680 K and outside room temperature 300 K.
Diesel engine is operating between the diesel burning temperature 873 K and outside room temperature 300 K.
Nicolas Leonard Sadi Carnot described a hypothetical engine known as Carnot engine.
The Carnot engine operates on an ideal reversible cycle between two heat reservoirs.
Carnot engine sets an upper limit on the efficiency of all other real engines.
Real engines are less efficient than Carnot engine because they do not operate through a reversible cycle.
Efficiency of real engines is further reduced due to friction and energy losses by conduction.
In a reversible process, the system undergoing the process can be returned to its initial condition along the same path on the p-V diagram.
The most important condition for a process to be reversible is that the process should be carried out infinitesimally slowly.
The Carnot cycle consists of four reversible processes; two isothermal processes and two adiabatic processes.
Step 1 of the Carnot cycle involves reversible isothermal expansion of the gas at the hot temperature $T_H$.
Step 2 of the Carnot cycle involves reversible adiabatic expansion, during which the gas cools to the cold temperature $T_C$.
Carnot's theorem states that no engine operating between two heat reservoirs can be more efficient than a Carnot engine operating between the same reservoirs.
